context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 4th March, 2024
Days Covered: 06 and 07 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Asymptotic Relationships [15 points]

(a) [7 pts] For each pair of functions f : ℕ+ → ℝ+ and g : ℕ+ → ℝ+ in the table below, list in the third column which of the following is the strongest or most specific asymptotic relationship that holds between f and g, from among these options:
    f ∼ g,   f ∈ Θ(g),   f ∈ O(g),   f ∈ o(g),   f ∈ ω(g),   f ∈ Ω(g),   None.
You do not need to explain your work in this part. (Assume that log means log2 if no base is specified.)

| f(n)                       | g(n)                       | Relationship? |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 6^n                        | n^1200                     |               |
| (log n)^6                  | 1200th-root of n           |               |
| log_6(n^1200)              | (log_1200 n)^6             |               |
| log(n!)                    | n log n                    |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉                 | 3^⌈log3 n⌉                 |               |
| 2^⌈log2 n⌉ − n + 1         | 3^⌈log3 n⌉ − n + 1         |               |
| n                          | n^(cos log n)              |               |

(b) [8 pts] Answer the same question for the pairs below, and justify your reasoning.

| f(n)             | g(n)             | Relationship? |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 2^((log n)^2)    | 2^(⌈log n⌉^2)    |               |
| 4^n              | (2n)! / (n!)^2   |               |


Problem 2. OEIS [10 points]

(a) [2 pts] Consider the sequence that begins with x0 = 0, x1 = 1, x2 = 2, and continues with xn = 5·x_{n−2} + 2·x_{n−3} for n ≥ 3. What are the values of x3, x4, x5, x6?
(b) [1 pt] Let's practice one of the most useful techniques when investigating a sequence or recurrence: checking whether someone else has already done it for you! The OEIS (Online Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences) is an invaluable tool that houses more than 300,000 noteworthy integer sequences together with descriptions, formulas, external references, relationships to other OEIS entries, etc.
Use https://oeis.org/ to look up an entry that might possibly match our sequence from part (a): type the values we've computed into their search bar, separated by commas. If you're not sure you've found the right one, you can gain confidence by computing more terms using our recurrence and seeing if they continue to match.
(c) [1 pt] Browse your chosen entry (scroll down to the "Formula" section) to find a possible closed form function of n that equals xn. Here, closed form means that the function can be computed directly from n without relying on other terms in the sequence or iterative processes such as Σ, Π, or "dot-dot-dot"s. Basic arithmetic functions such as exponents are fine.
    Note: lines that include the acronyms "g.f." or "e.g.f." (generating function and exponential generating function) can be safely ignored for this problem.
(d) [6 pts] Don't trust everything you read online! Use induction to carefully prove the formula you found in the previous part.


Problem 3. Bounded Asymptotics [15 points]

In this problem, we investigate the asymptotic relationship of functions under some conditions. You can assume that any function mentioned in the problem maps ℕ to ℝ+. You might find the following definitions useful:
1. A function f is bounded from below (respectively above) if there exists a real number B such that f(n) ≥ B (respectively f(n) ≤ B) for all n ∈ ℕ.
2. A function f is bounded away from zero if there exists a real number B > 0 such that f(n) ≥ B for all n ∈ ℕ.
3. A function f is strictly increasing (respectively decreasing) if for any a, b ∈ ℕ we have that a > b ⇒ f(a) > f(b) (respectively a > b ⇒ f(a) < f(b)).

(a) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded away from zero. Carefully prove that f ∈ O(g).
    Hint: Careful! The limit lim_{n→∞} f(n)/g(n) might not exist, so your argument shouldn't use limits.
(b) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is bounded from above and g be a function that is bounded from below. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)?
(c) [5 pts] Let f be a function that is strictly decreasing and g be a function that is strictly increasing. Is it necessarily true that f ∈ O(g)? Is it necessarily true that f ∈ o(g)?


Problem 4. Karatsuba's Algorithm [10 points]

As we'll see soon, cryptography often requires arithmetic with large integers, possibly with hundreds or thousands of digits each! When adding two integers A and B each with n digits, the straightforward algorithm requires at most 2n single-digit additions. But the straightforward multiplication algorithm involves multiplying each digit of A with each digit of B, which is already Θ(n^2) operations. Karatsuba's Algorithm is a clever divide-and-conquer algorithm with (hopefully) faster asymptotic runtime.

Karatsuba's Algorithm can multiply two n-digit numbers using at most T(n) single-digit arithmetic operations, where T(n) is defined by the following recurrence:
    T(0) = 0,   T(1) = 1,   T(n) = 3·T(⌈n/2⌉) + 10n   for n ≥ 2.

(a) [1 pt] Compute T(2), T(3), and T(4).
(b) [4 pts] Use the Plug and Chug method to find an exact (not just asymptotic!) closed-form expression for T(n), assuming n is an exact power of 2. You do not need to prove your formula, but you should check it against T(1), T(2), and T(4).
    Note: We'd like you to use the Plug and Chug method specifically; solutions that arrive at a closed form using other techniques (or without explanation) will only receive partial credit.
(c) [4 pts] Karatsuba's algorithm works whether or not n is a power of 2, and the same recurrence applies, so let's find an approximate solution for T(n) in this more general case: carefully use the Master Theorem to prove an asymptotic bound of the form T(n) ∈ Θ(g(n)), for some closed-form function g(n).
(d) [1 pt] Is Karatsuba an asymptotic improvement over the straightforward Θ(n^2) algorithm? Why or why not?

Optional Note: Here's a rough description of Karatsuba's Algorithm, in case you're curious. If A and B are n-digit numbers, we can split each list of digits in half by writing A = A0·X + A1 and B = B0·X + B1, where X = 10^⌈n/2⌉. We could compute A · B using the formula
    A · B = [A0·B0]·X^2 + ([A0·B1] + [A1·B0])·X + [A1·B1],
which requires 4 multiplications of ⌈n/2⌉-digit numbers, leading to T(n) = 4·T(⌈n/2⌉) + O(n) with solution Θ(n^2). Karatsuba cleverly observed that P, Q, R can be computed with just three multiplications:
    P = [A0·B0]
    R = [A1·B1]
    Q = P + R − [(A0 − A1)·(B0 − B1)].
This reduces the 4 to a 3 in the recurrence, while the O(n) term remains O(n), which directly leads to the asymptotic speedup.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this pset honestly. Could you just explain the main ideas from the beginning, especially the asymptotics and recurrence parts?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — psets can feel overwhelming at first! But my role is to help you work through specific problems, not to re-teach the material from scratch. That's what your lectures, recitation, and textbook are for.

So let's dig in together. Which problem or part would you like to start with? Pick one and tell me your first instinct about how to approach it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the material rather than engaging with a specific problem. My role is to guide them through the assignment, not to lecture them on course content. I should redirect them to a specific problem and ask them to start working on it, using the Socratic method to help them discover the ideas themselves. I'll be friendly but firm about my role.

exchanges.student: Maybe Problem 1, but I’m not really sure where to even start. I keep mixing up all the symbols like \(O\), \(o\), \(\Theta\), and \(\sim\), so the whole table kind of blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start small. Without looking anything up — what's your intuition about the difference between f ∈ O(g) and f ∼ g? Even a rough, informal sense is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing confusion about the asymptotic notation symbols. Rather than explaining all of them, I should use the Socratic method to probe what they already know, starting with the most basic distinction. I'll ask them a targeted question to get them to articulate what they do know, which will help me identify the specific gap and guide them from there. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think \(O(g)\) means \(f\) is like not bigger than \(g\) up to a constant, and \(f \sim g\) means they’re basically equal somehow? But then I get stuck because \(\Theta\) and \(o\) start feeling the same too, and then I’m lost again.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts! You're on the right track with both. Now, think about this: if f ∼ g, what does that tell you about the ratio f(n)/g(n) as n → ∞? And what does f ∈ Θ(g) tell you about that same ratio?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition for O and ~, which is good. I should acknowledge this briefly and then push them to be more precise about the distinction between ~ and Θ, since that's where they're getting stuck. Rather than explaining the difference myself, I'll ask a probing question that moves toward the formal definitions. This will help them realize the key distinction: ~ is stronger than Θ because it pins down the exact constant (ratio → 1), while Θ only requires the ratio to be bounded.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember something with the ratio going to 1 for \(f \sim g\), but for \(\Theta\) I just remember constants and inequalities, not what the ratio does. Could you maybe just give me the big picture of all the asymptotic relationships together, because I keep forgetting how they compare?
exchanges.tutor:
You've actually got the key insight for ~: the ratio goes to 1. That's exactly right.

Now, for Θ: if the ratio f(n)/g(n) doesn't necessarily go to 1, but it stays bounded, what does that mean? Think about what 'bounded' means for a ratio — what values can it take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a full overview of all asymptotic relationships, which would be me re-teaching course content rather than guiding them through the problem. I should redirect them and stay Socratic. However, they did correctly identify that f ~ g means the ratio goes to 1, which is great. I should acknowledge that and push them to think about what Θ means for the ratio, rather than giving them the full picture. Keep it focused and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I guess it stays between two constants? But then I’m still not sure how that helps me do the rows in the table, because some of them look weird and I don’t know which comparison rule I’m supposed to use first. Could you just walk through how I should think about a table like this from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on Θ — the ratio stays sandwiched between two positive constants. You now have the key ideas for both ~ and Θ.

Let's just dive in. Look at the first row: f(n) = 6^n and g(n) = n^1200. What happens to the ratio f(n)/g(n) as n → ∞? Just think about what you know about exponential vs. polynomial growth.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a general walkthrough rather than engaging with a specific problem. They did correctly identify that Θ means the ratio is bounded between two constants, which I should acknowledge briefly. However, I need to redirect them to actually attempt a specific row in the table rather than asking for a general methodology. The Socratic approach here is to pick the simplest row and ask them to try it, rather than giving them a framework. This will be more productive than a general explanation.

exchanges.student: I know exponentials beat polynomials, so I think \(6^n / n^{1200}\) goes to infinity. But then I freeze on what the “strongest” label is supposed to be, and I’m not sure if that means \(\omega\) or just \(\Omega\).
exchanges.tutor: Good — the ratio goes to infinity. Now think about it this way: if f/g → ∞, does that make f ∈ Ω(g) true, false, or something else? And does it make f ∈ ω(g) true? Which of those two is the stronger statement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the ratio goes to infinity, which is great progress. Now they need to connect that observation to the correct asymptotic notation. I should ask them a targeted question: if the ratio goes to infinity, what does that rule out, and what does it imply? This will help them reason their way to ω being stronger than Ω, rather than me just telling them.

exchanges.student: I think both are true, and \(\omega(g)\) is stronger. But I’m worried I’m going to mess up the other rows that aren’t as obvious, like the ceiling/log ones or the factorial one, so can you maybe just explain the overall strategy for all the different types?
exchanges.tutor:
Correct — ω(g) is the answer and you reasoned it out perfectly.

The best strategy is to keep doing exactly what you just did: ask what the ratio does. Try the fourth row now: f(n) = log(n!) and g(n) = n log n. What do you know about log(n!)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly concluded that ω is the answer for row 1 and understands why it's stronger than Ω. They keep asking for a general strategy overview, which I should decline - my role is to guide them through specific problems. I've now seen this request for a general explanation 3-4 times. I should acknowledge their correct answer, be firm about not giving a general walkthrough, and push them to try the next row themselves. The log(n!) row is a good candidate since it involves a well-known result they likely encountered in class.

exchanges.student: I kind of remember it’s close to \(n \log n\), maybe from Stirling or something, but I don’t remember whether that makes it \(\Theta\) or \(\sim\). I’m honestly still feeling like I need the whole picture, not just one row at a time.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the full overview — working through specific examples is exactly how the picture will become clear to you.

You're close on log(n!). Stirling's approximation says log(n!) ∼ n log n. Given your definitions of ∼ and Θ from earlier, which is the stronger statement here: f ∼ g or f ∈ Θ(g)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the whole picture. This is now the fourth or fifth time they've made this request. I should be firm and redirect. They correctly recalled that log(n!) ~ n log n, which is actually the key insight. I should push them to be more precise: if they know log(n!) is 'close to' n log n, can they say whether the ratio goes to exactly 1, or just stays bounded? This will help them distinguish ~ from Θ for this row. I should also be a bit more direct about not providing the full overview.

exchanges.student: I think \(\sim\) is stronger than \(\Theta\). But then for the weird rows I still won’t know what to do, like \(2^{\lceil \log_2 n\rceil}\) versus \(3^{\lceil \log_3 n\rceil}\), or the one with the minus \(n+1\). Could we switch to one of those because those are the ones making me feel totally lost?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's go there. Start with row 5: f(n) = 2^⌈log₂ n⌉. The ceiling makes it look scary, but think about it this way — what is ⌈log₂ n⌉ in relation to log₂ n? And what does that tell you about how 2^⌈log₂ n⌉ compares to n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that ~ is stronger than Θ, which is great. They want to move to the ceiling rows, which is a legitimate request since those are genuinely harder and different in character. I should let them move there. The key insight for row 5 (2^⌈log2 n⌉ vs 3^⌈log3 n⌉) is that 2^⌈log2 n⌉ is always between n and 2n (i.e., Θ(n)), and similarly 3^⌈log3 n⌉ is Θ(n). So both are Θ(n), making them Θ of each other. I should guide the student to discover this by asking what 2^⌈log2 n⌉ simplifies to or what range it's in.

exchanges.student: I know \(\lceil \log_2 n\rceil\) is between \(\log_2 n\) and \(\log_2 n + 1\), so \(2^{\lceil \log_2 n\rceil}\) is between \(n\) and \(2n\). I’m still not seeing the full comparison to the base-3 version though, and then the next row with subtracting \(n-1\) makes it even worse.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — great work! Now apply the same reasoning to 3^⌈log₃ n⌉. What bounds can you put on it? Once you have bounds for both, what can you say about their ratio?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made excellent progress - they correctly bounded 2^⌈log₂ n⌉ between n and 2n. Now I should guide them to apply the same reasoning to 3^⌈log₃ n⌉, and then ask them what the ratio of the two functions looks like given those bounds. This is a natural next step. I should not jump to the n-1 row yet - let them finish this one first.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the tutor states 'Stirling's approximation says log(n!) ∼ n log n' — this is the key reasoning step (connecting the approximation to the asymptotic relationship) that the student should have been guided to derive or recall themselves. The tutor gives the bridge from evidence to conclusion directly rather than asking the student to recall or work out what Stirling implies.

grade.overview: The tutor does a strong job of using Socratic questioning throughout, refusing to give broad overviews and instead guiding the student row by row. The main deduction is in Turn 8, where the tutor directly supplies the Stirling approximation result (log(n!) ∼ n log n) rather than prompting the student to recall or derive it. This is a key reasoning step that the student had already partially identified ('I kind of remember it's close to n log n, maybe from Stirling'), so the tutor could have asked the student to complete that thought. The deduction is applied under 1.1.C.b. All other criteria are met well: scaffolding builds on student progress, responses are concise and well-toned, and the conversation stays on-task.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor consistently uses guided questioning and avoids giving submission-ready answers or rewriting student work. The one notable lapse is in Turn 8, where the tutor directly states 'Stirling's approximation says log(n!) ∼ n log n' — the student had already hinted at knowing Stirling, so the tutor should have asked the student to complete the reasoning rather than supplying the key fact. This triggers 1.1.C.b (tutor does the key reasoning step). No other rubric deductions are warranted: scaffolding is solid, responses are bite-sized, tone is supportive, and the conversation stays anchored to the assignment.
